questions.stimulus_text: Arts advocate: Few universities require that students who are specializing in science and technology take many art courses. However, real progress in science and technology requires an element of creativity, which the arts foster. Thus, to help science and technology students succeed in their careers, universities should increase the number of art courses required for them.
questions.question_text: Which of the following would, if true, most strengthen the argument above?

options.a: Universities required more art courses for science and technology students in the past.
options.b: Participation in art courses increases students' creative thinking in their science and technology courses.
options.c: More students who are interested in art would specialize in science and technology if the number of art courses required for those programs were increased.
options.d: Some of the most prominent scientists began their careers as artists.
options.e: Discussion of science and technology topics in art courses increases creative thinking among students in those courses.

questions.stimulus_text: Ramirez: The film industry claims that pirated DVDs, which are usually cheaper than legitimate DVDs and become available well before a film's official DVD release date, adversely affect its bottom line. But the industry should note what the spread of piracy indicates: consumers want lower prices and faster DVD releases. Lowering prices of DVDs and releasing them sooner would mitigate piracy's negative effect on film industry profits.
questions.question_text: The argument above relies on which of the following assumptions?

options.a: Releasing legitimate DVDs earlier would not cause any reduction in the revenue the film industry receives from the films' theatrical release.
options.b: Some people who would otherwise purchase pirated DVDs would be willing to purchase legitimate DVDs if they were less expensive and released earlier than they are now.
options.c: The film industry will in the future be able to produce DVDs more cheaply than is currently the case.
options.d: Some producers of pirated DVDs would likely discontinue their businesses if legitimate DVDs were released faster and priced lower.
options.e: Current purchasers of pirated DVDs are aware that those DVDs are not authorized by the film industry.

questions.stimulus_text: Harunia Province has a relatively dry climate and is attracting a fast-growing population that has put increasing demands on its water supply. The two companies that supply water to the region have struggled to keep up with demand and still remain profitable. Yet now they are asking Harunian authorities to write residential water-use regulations that could reduce their revenues and restrict their future flexibility in supplying water profitably.
questions.question_text: Which of the following would, if true, most logically help explain why the water-supply companies are asking the authorities to regulate residential water use?

options.a: The companies are planning large water-transportation and irrigation systems that require the approval of neighboring provinces.
options.b: The companies believe regulation is inevitable and that having it in place now will allow better planning and thus future profitability.
options.c: Few, if any, Harunian government officials have investments in the companies or serve on their boards of directors.
options.d: The companies believe that the population is not likely to continue to grow.
options.e: Long-term climate projections suggest that greater rainfall will eventually increase the amount of water available.

questions.stimulus_text: Loss of the Gocha mangrove forests has caused coastal erosion, reducing fish populations and requiring the Gocha Fishing Cooperative (GFC) to partially fund dredging and new shore facilities. However, one of its subsidiary businesses, the GFC has now invested in a program to replant significant parts of the coast with mangrove trees. Given income from a controlled harvest of wood with continuing replanting, the mangrove regeneration effort makes it more likely that the cooperative will increase its net income.
questions.question_text: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument that mangrove replanting will increase the Gocha cooperative's net income?

options.a: The cost of dredging and shore facilities was shared with the local government.
options.b: The GFC will be able to hire local workers to assist with the mangrove replanting.
options.c: The GFC derives 10 percent of its revenue from salt-production facilities in an area previously cleared of mangroves.
options.d: Mangrove forests tend to increase the commercial fish populations in coastal fishing grounds.
options.e: A controlled harvesting of mangrove wood by the GFC would have little effect on coastal erosion.

questions.stimulus_text: Executives at the Fizzles Beverage Company plan to boost profits in Country X on their range of fruit-flavored drinks by introducing new flavors based on tropical fruits that are little known there. The executives reason that since the fruit drinks of other companies have none of these flavors, Fizzles will not have to compete for customers and thus will be able to sell the drinks at a higher price.
questions.question_text: Which of the following, if true, presents the most serious potential weakness of the plan?

options.a: The new fruit drinks would be priced significantly higher than other Fizzles fruit drinks with more conventional flavors.
options.b: In a telephone survey, at least one of the consumers contacted said that they preferred many of the new flavors to all of the more familiar flavors.
options.c: To build widespread demand for the new flavors, Fizzles would have to launch an advertising campaign to familiarize consumers with them.
options.d: Consumers choosing among fruit-flavored drinks of different brands generally buy on the basis of name recognition and price rather than the specific fruit flavor.
options.e: Few consumers who are loyal to a specific brand of fruit-flavored drinks would willingly switch to another brand that costs more.

questions.stimulus_text: Economist: In 2015, the average per-person amount paid for goods and services purchased by consumers in Country X was the equivalent of $17,570 in United States dollars, just 30 percent of the corresponding figure of $58,620 for Country Y. Yet, in 2015, there was already a substantial middle class in Country X that had discretionary income for middle-class consumer goods such as new vehicles, computers, or major household appliances, while a significant portion of the middle class in Country Y did not have sufficient income to purchase such items.
questions.question_text: Which of the following, if true, most helps explain the discrepancy in the relationships described by the economist?

options.a: There are many consumer goods, such as household appliances, that are produced in Country X to be sold in the Country Y market.
options.b: The volume of trade between Country X and Country Y is increasing rapidly in both directions.
options.c: The economy of Country Y is recovering from a downturn that affected both Country Y and Country X.
options.d: Country X residents pay much less than their Country Y counterparts for housing, transportation, and child care.
options.e: In Country Y as well as in Country X, there are few assembly-line jobs in factories that pay a middle-class wage.

questions.stimulus_text: Neuroscientist: Memory evolved to help animals react appropriately to situations they encounter by drawing on the past experience of similar situations. But this does not require that animals perfectly recall every detail of all their experiences. Instead, to function well, memory should generalize from past experiences that are similar to the current one.
questions.question_text: The neuroscientist's statements, if true, most strongly support which of the following conclusions?

options.a: At least some animals perfectly recall every detail of at least some past experiences.
options.b: Perfectly recalling every detail of all their past experiences could help at least some animals react more appropriately than they otherwise would to new situations they encounter.
options.c: Generalizing from past experiences requires clear memories of most if not all the details of those experiences.
options.d: Recalling every detail of all past experiences would be incompatible with any ability to generalize from those experiences.
options.e: Animals can often react more appropriately than they otherwise would to situations they encounter if they draw on generalizations from past experiences of similar situations.

questions.stimulus_text: In Country X's last election, the Reform Party beat its main opponent, the Conservative Party, although pollsters, employing in-person interviews shortly before the vote, had projected a Conservative Party victory. Afterward, the pollsters determined that, unlike Conservative Party supporters, Reform Party supporters were less likely to express their party preference during in-person interviews than they were during telephone interviews. Therefore, using only telephone interviews instead would likely result in more accurate projections for the next election.
questions.question_text: Which of the following statements, if true, would most support the argument in the passage?

options.a: The number of voters in Country X's next election will be significantly larger than the number of voters in the last election.
options.b: The Conservative Party will win the next election.
options.c: For each person interviewed in telephone polls before the next election, pollsters will be able to reasonably determine the likelihood of that person voting.
options.d: People who expressed no party preference during the in-person interviews shortly before Country X's last election did not outnumber the people who expressed a preference for the Conservative Party.
options.e: In the next election, pollsters will be able to conduct more in-person interviews than telephone interviews.

questions.stimulus_text: A company that manufactures plastic products from recyclable plastic is, surprisingly, unconcerned that economic conditions may worsen, despite analysts' belief that consumers would then consider ecofriendly plastic products an expensive luxury. But the company reasons that it will be able to lower its prices because, in a weakened economy, other ecofriendly plastic manufacturers are likely to fail. Demand among manufacturers for recyclable plastics as raw materials would then plummet, creating an oversupply of such materials, making them less expensive for the manufacturer to purchase and thus lowering the company's costs.
questions.question_text: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's reasoning?

options.a: Smaller ecofriendly plastic manufacturers are more likely to fail in a weakened economy than larger ecofriendly manufacturers are.
options.b: Some retailers whose sales include various companies' ecofriendly plastic products have struggled in recent years despite the overall good economy.
options.c: Consumers would likely soon learn of the oversupply of recyclable plastics and cease recycling them, significantly raising manufacturers' raw-material costs.
options.d: Retailers, including retailers that cater to consumers seeking certain types of ecofriendly products, may lose some business if economic conditions worsen.
options.e: The plastics used in the company in its products were, after a recent investigation by a regulatory body, declared to be safe for consumers.

questions.stimulus_text: Researchers asked volunteers to imagine they were running a five-kilometer race against 50 people and then against 500 people, races in each of which the top 10 percent would receive a $1,000 prize. Asked about the effort they would apply in the respective cases, the volunteers indicated, on average, that they would run slower in the race against the greater number of people. A likely explanation of this result is that those of the volunteers who were most comparatively inclined—those who most tended to compare themselves with others in the social environment—determined (perhaps unconsciously) that extreme effort would not be worthwhile in the 500-competitor race.
questions.question_text: Which of the following would, if known to be true, most help justify the explanation offered above?

options.a: The volunteers who were most comparatively inclined were also those who had the greatest desire to win a $1,000 prize.
options.b: The volunteers who were the least comparatively inclined had no greater desire to win the $1,000 than those who were the most comparatively inclined.
options.c: The volunteers who were most comparatively inclined were likely to indicate that they would run the two races at the same speed.
options.d: The most comparatively inclined volunteers believed that they were significantly less likely to finish in the top 10 percent in the race against 500 than in the race against 50.
options.e: Volunteers were chosen for participation in the study on the basis of answers to various questions designed to measure the degree to which the volunteers were comparatively inclined.

questions.stimulus_text: According to a study, after a week of high-altitude living, twenty men had slimmed down. The men, middle-aged residents of low-altitude areas, had been taken to a research station at 2,650 meters (8,694 feet) above sea level. They had unrestricted access to food and were forbidden vigorous exercise, yet they lost an average of 1.5 kilograms (3.3 pounds) during their one-week stay. Clearly, the lower availability of oxygen at higher altitudes, or hypobaric hypoxia, can be said to have caused the weight loss, since __________.
questions.question_text: Which of the following would, if true, most logically complete the argument?

options.a: a decrease in oxygen intake has been shown to depress appetite
options.b: the men all participated in the same kinds of exercise during their stay
options.c: the foods available to the men had fewer calories than the foods they usually ate
options.d: exercise at higher altitudes is more difficult than exercise at lower altitudes is
options.e: several weeks after returning home, the men still weighed less than they had before the study

questions.stimulus_text: Editorial: Our city's public transportation agency is facing a budget shortfall. The fastest-growing part of the budget has been employee retirement benefits, which are exceptionally generous. Unless the budget shortfall is resolved, transportation service will be cut, and many transportation employees will lose their jobs. Thus, it would be in the employees' best interest for their union to accept cuts in retirement benefits.
questions.question_text: Which of the following is an assumption the editorial's argument requires?

options.a: The transportation employees' union should not accept cuts in retirement benefits if doing so would not be in the employees' best interest.
options.b: The only feasible way for the agency to resolve the budget shortfall would involve cutting transportation service and eliminating jobs.
options.c: Other things being equal, it is in the transportation employees' interest to have exceptionally generous retirement benefits.
options.d: Cutting the retirement benefits would help resolve the agency's budget shortfall.
options.e: The transportation employees' union will not accept cuts in retirement benefits if doing so will not allow more transportation employees to keep their jobs.

questions.stimulus_text: Researchers hope to find clues about the A'mk peoples who lived in the Kaumpta region about one thousand years ago but who left few obvious traces. The researchers plan to hire the few remaining shamans of the modern-day indigenous people in Kaumpta, who are believed to be descended from the A'mk, to lead them to ancestral sites that may be the remains of A'mk buildings or ceremonial spaces. The shamans were taught the location of such sites as part of their traditional training as youths, and their knowledge of traditional Kaumpta customs may help determine the nature of any sites the researchers find.
questions.question_text: Which of the following is an assumption on which the success of the plan depends?

options.a: The researchers have reliable evidence that the A'mk of one thousand years ago built important ceremonial spaces.
options.b: The shamans have a reasonably accurate memory of A'mk sites they learned about as youths.
options.c: Kaumpta shamans are generally held in high esteem for their traditional knowledge.
options.d: Modern technologies available to the researchers are likely to be able to find some A'mk sites easily.
options.e: Most or all A'mk sites are likely to be found within the Kaumpta region.

questions.stimulus_text: Astronomer: Most stars are born in groups of thousands, each star in a group forming from the same parent cloud of gas. Each cloud has a unique, homogeneous chemical composition. Therefore, whenever two stars have the same chemical composition as each other, they must have originated from the same cloud of gas.
questions.question_text: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the astronomer's argument?

options.a: In some groups of stars, not every star originated from the same parent cloud of gas.
options.b: Clouds of gas of similar or identical chemical composition may be remote from each other.
options.c: Whenever a star forms, it inherits the chemical composition of its parent cloud of gas.
options.d: Many stars in vastly different parts of the universe are quite similar in their chemical compositions.
options.e: Astronomers can at least sometimes precisely determine whether a star has the same chemical composition as its parent cloud of gas.

questions.stimulus_text: With employer-paid training, workers have the potential to become more productive not only in their present employment but also in any number of jobs with different employers. To increase the productivity of their workforce, many firms are planning to maintain or even increase their investments in worker training. But some training experts object that if a trained worker is hired away by another firm, the employer that paid for the training has merely subsidized a competitor. They note that such hiring has been on the rise in recent years.
questions.question_text: Which of the following would, if true, contribute most to defeating the training experts' objection to the firms' strategy?

options.a: Firms that promise opportunities for advancement to their employees get, on average, somewhat larger numbers of job applications from untrained workers than do firms that make no such promise.
options.b: In many industries, employees who take continuing-education courses are more competitive in the job market.
options.c: More and more educational and training institutions are offering reduced tuition fees to firms that subsidize worker training.
options.d: Research shows that workers whose training is wholly or partially subsidized by their employer tend to get at least as much training as do workers who pay for all their own training.
options.e: For most firms that invest in training their employees, the value added by that investment in employees who stay exceeds the value lost through other employees' leaving to work for other companies.

questions.stimulus_text: In emerging economies in Africa and other regions, large foreign banks that were set up during the colonial era have long played a major economic role. These institutions have tended to confine their business to the wealthier of banks' potential customers. But development of these countries' economies requires financing of the small businesses that dominate their manufacturing, farming, and services sectors. So economic growth will be likely to occur if local banks take on this portion of the financial services markets, since __________.
questions.question_text: Which of the following completions would produce the strongest argument?

options.a: local banks tend not to strive as much as large foreign banks to diversify their investments
options.b: small farming and manufacturing businesses contribute to economic growth if they obtain adequate investment capital
options.c: large foreign banks in emerging economies could, with local employees and appropriate local consultation, profitably expand their business to less wealthy clients
options.d: some small businesses are among the wealthier customers of foreign banks in emerging economies
options.e: local banks in emerging economies tend to be less risk-averse than foreign banks

questions.stimulus_text: Exporters in Country X are facing lower revenues due to a shortage of the large metal shipping containers in which they send their goods by sea to other countries. Fewer containers arrive in Country X due to reductions in imports. This has meant lost orders, costly delays, and a scramble for alternatives, such as air freight, all of which are costlier. Moreover, the revenues of exporters in Country X will probably continue to decline in the near future. This is because other countries are likely to find it increasingly unprofitable to export their goods to Country X, and because __________.
questions.question_text: Which of the following would most logically complete the passage?

options.a: production of shipping containers in Country X is growing rapidly as a response to the shortage
options.b: shipping companies are willing to move containers from country to country only when the containers are full
options.c: the cost of shipping alternatives such as air freight is likely to stabilize in the near future
options.d: consumers in Country X are purchasing more products than ever before
options.e: the worldwide demand for goods made in Country X has only recently begun to rise after a long decline

questions.stimulus_text: Candle Corporation's television stations are likely to have more income from advertisers than previously. This is because advertisers prefer to reach people in the 18- to 49-year-old age group and the number of people in that group watching Candle television is increasing. Furthermore, among Candle viewers, the percentage of viewers 18 to 49 years old is increasing.
questions.question_text: Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the argument that Candle Corporation will receive more income from advertisers?

options.a: Advertisers carefully monitor the demographic characteristics of television audiences and purchase advertising time to reach the audiences they prefer to reach.
options.b: Among people over 49 years old, fewer viewers of Candle stations buy products advertised on television than do viewers of other stations.
options.c: There will be increasingly more advertisements on television that are directed at viewers who are over 49 years old.
options.d: Candle stations plan to show reruns of television shows during hours when other stations run shows for the first time.
options.e: People 18 to 49 years old generally have less disposable income to spend than do people over 49 years old.
